nativeRange.habitat: Main channel and floodplain reaches of the Mekong River and associated tributaries; moderate to fast-flowing water over fine sand, silt, and mixed sediment substrates; often associated with sandy banks and marginal slow-water pockets where ambush positions are available

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (8 cm minimum — mandatory for burial behaviour)
minimal or no gravel (sharp substrate injures burrowing fish)
driftwood and smooth rocks for visual cover and territory demarcation
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) to mimic natural biotope
low to moderate flow with calmer sandy pockets replicating natural ambush sites
subdued lighting; floating plants or surface cover to diffuse overhead light

diet.notes: Obligate live-prey ambush predator; weaning onto prepared or frozen foods is very rarely achieved and cannot be expected. Staple diet of live invertebrates: pond snails, ramshorn snails, mystery snails (hard shells wear down continuously-growing beak teeth), live ghost shrimp, river shrimp, and earthworms. Live feeder fish (small minnows, guppies) are accepted but nutritionally incomplete as a sole food and carry disease risk; limit to occasional variety. Gut-loaded invertebrates preferred. Hard-shelled snails are essential to prevent dental overgrowth — the beak grows continuously and will require veterinary trimming without adequate hard prey. Feed every 2–3 days; overfeeding causes obesity. Starved specimens rapidly decline.

avoidWith: all other fish species (ambush predator that will attack any tankmate regardless of size)
invertebrates (snails and shrimp are used as live food only, not permanent tankmates)
conspecifics (extreme intraspecific aggression; multiple individuals cannot be safely housed together)
any bottom-dwelling species sharing the substrate layer

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Tetraodon baileyi; should show characteristic fleshy filamentous skin projections. Verify current availability before use.
Wikimedia Commons candidate; verify image exists and confirm license before use. Shows hairy skin filaments if available.

summary: The Hairy Puffer is a rare Mekong endemic and the only pufferfish species bearing fleshy hair-like skin filaments across its entire body. A large ambush predator reaching ~18 cm, it buries in fine sand awaiting prey and demands a specialist single-fish setup with live foods exclusively.
careNotes:
The Hairy Puffer (Tetraodon baileyi) is one of the rarest and most unusual freshwater pufferfish in the hobby, distinguished from all other pufferfish by a dense covering of fleshy, hair-like dermal filaments (fimbriae) that cover the body — an adaptation unique within the family Tetraodontidae that provides exceptional camouflage against sandy substrates. It is endemic to the Mekong River drainage of Thailand, Cambodia, and Laos and is infrequently collected due to its patchy distribution in a large, difficult-to-access river system.

Like the closely related Congo Puffer (Tetraodon miurus), the Hairy Puffer is a sit-and-wait ambush predator that buries itself in fine sand with only the eyes and mouth exposed, lunging explosively upward or laterally to engulf passing prey. This strategy demands a mandatory minimum of 8 cm of very fine-grained sand (grain size under 1 mm) across the entire tank footprint. Coarser substrate prevents burial, causes chronic stress, and will injure the abdomen and skin filaments. No tankmate is compatible — this species must be housed alone without exception.

Adult size typically reaches 18–19 cm total length, making minimum tank volume of 200 L with a 120 cm footprint a practical lower bound for a single adult. Excellent filtration is non-negotiable: puffers are heavy waste producers and are highly sensitive to ammonia and nitrite. Weekly partial water changes of 30–50% are recommended. Flow should be moderate with accessible low-flow pockets; the Mekong channel is not uniformly fast, and the fish seeks sheltered ambush sites.

Water parameters should replicate the slightly soft, near-neutral conditions of the Mekong: pH 6.5–7.8, gH 5–15, kH 2–8, temperature 24–28°C. Extreme parameters in either direction are poorly tolerated.

The diet is strictly live prey; hard-shelled snails (ramshorn, pond, mystery) must form the backbone of the feeding regimen to prevent beak overgrowth. Beak teeth grow continuously and will require veterinary trimming if not worn by hard prey. Feed every 2–3 days rather than daily; obesity from overfeeding is a common cause of premature death in captive specimens. Feeder fish carry disease risks and should be quarantined or sourced from disease-free cultures.

The hairy skin filaments are diagnostically important and should be monitored during water quality checks — frayed, damaged, or absent filaments can indicate physical damage, skin disease, or chronic stress. Like all Tetraodontidae, this species contains tetrodotoxin concentrated in the skin and organs; always handle with gloves and dispose of deceased specimens safely. Puffing up is a severe stress indicator and must not be deliberately triggered.

Acquisition is challenging: Tetraodon baileyi is rarely exported and commands high prices when available. Wild-caught specimens may carry internal parasites and should be quarantined for at least four weeks with prophylactic treatment under veterinary guidance before introduction to a display tank.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Tetraodon baileyi has not been documented in the mainstream aquarium literature. The extreme intraspecific aggression characteristic of large Tetraodon species makes pairing attempts highly dangerous and unlikely to succeed without substantial purpose-built facilities. This species is considered not bred in captivity for practical hobby purposes.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tetraodon baileyi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 19 cm), temperature range, pH (6.5–7.8), hardness, native range (Mekong drainage: Thailand, Cambodia, Laos), habitat description (sandy substrate, main river channel), diet (live invertebrates; live-food-only in practice), minimum tank size, substrate requirements (fine sand, deep bed mandatory), aggression notes, and single-species tank requirement. Also described the unique dermal filament (‘hairy’) morphology as unique within Tetraodontidae.

additional.name: FishBase — Tetraodon baileyi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Tetraodontidae, Tetraodontiformes), adult size (to 19 cm TL per Roberts 1982 original description), native range (Mekong basin), habitat, and temperature tolerance. Confirmed correct order and family. Verified species is known from Thailand, Cambodia, and Laos reaches of the Mekong River system.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Puffer Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for general freshwater pufferfish husbandry context: live-food diet, beak overgrowth management, snail-based dental wear, and single-species tank requirements. Hairy Puffer not covered as a dedicated species; general puffer guidance cross-referenced with species-specific data.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Hairy Puffer (Tetraodon baileyi)
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming ambush predator behaviour, mandatory fine-sand substrate depth, unique hairy skin filament morphology, and specialist-only single-fish care requirements. Corroborated live-food-only diet and rarity of the species in the hobby.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine — Mekong Pufferfish
additional.notes: Used for Mekong biotope context, description of sand-burying ambush behaviour specific to T. baileyi, dermal filament camouflage function, and specialist-level care requirements. Corroborated 8 cm+ sand depth, live-feeder-only diet, and endemic status within the Mekong drainage.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive spawning records exist. Extreme intraspecific aggression prevents pairing attempts under standard aquarium conditions.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Rare Mekong endemic ambush predator; aggressive single-fish species-only setup required (no viable tankmates). Reaches 18+cm with specialized buried-ambush behavior and live-feeder-only diet.
